title: 2026/02/11 - GA [Part 2]
displayTitle: 2026-02-10 - Governance and Administration Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of Taumata Arowai - the Water Services Regulator [Part 2]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of Taumata Arowai - the Water Services Regulator
description: 2 February 2026 | Governance and Administration Committee 2024/25 Annual review of Taumata Arowai - the Water Services Regulator Taumata Arowai Raveen Jaduram, Chair Allan Prangnell, Chief Executive Sarah Howard, Head of Sector Advice and Relations Sara McFall, Head of Systems, Strategy, and Performance Steve Taylor, Head of Regulatory Yazmin Juned Bevan Tydda Zoe Ogilvie-Burns
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of Taumata Arowai - the Water Services Regulator. Taumata Arowai. Raveen Jaduram, Chair

chunks.text: Thanks, the governance and administrative party. Um, we are interested to speak to you today and we'll for 45 minutes. So, uh, we will give you opportunity to make some opening comments, but if you could just maybe introduce yourselves, introduce the team, and if any, um, other colleagues want to come up to the table, you're more than welcome.

Uh, but we just please ask that you just introduce yourself. Um, and, uh, so just so we know who's, uh, speaking for the record. And we will give you opportunity to, to make some opening comments now, and then we'll move to questions from you. So, hope To you thank you very much, and we, we appreciate the time and we understand time is important. So, um, I'll keep my comments brief.

Um, my name is Ravi Jaan. I'm the chair of, uh, Tam, ROI, the Water Services Authority. I've got, uh, Alan Pregno here, who's the CEO, and he will introduce the risk of his team. Um, the organization is very new. It's only established a few years ago, and its first task was to get the drinking water regulations in place, but with the local water done well, legislation, suite of legislation last year.

Our role has changed. We've got more a leadership role now with some oversight in addition to drinking water of stormwater and wastewater. Uh, but with those comments, ask to Alan. Thanks Ravi, and thanks for the opportunity, um, this morning. Just wanna introduce the team here.

So we've got, um, we've got Steve, who is head of operations and, uh, with, uh, with accountability and responsibility over the drinking water system primarily, uh, and the frontline offices. And, and we've got Sarah who, uh, is, uh, head of strategy and performance and is picking up quite a lot of the new tools and responsibilities out of local water done well, reforms like standardization of infrastructure and the, the, the tools that we use, uh, as a regulator and behind me, um, I've got, uh, Sarah, who, uh, is head

of our external relations area. And of course, uh, everyone drinks water. Uh, and so we have a lot of, uh, relationships across the country and very many diverse communities to, to serve. Uh, Bevin here is our CFO. Um, and, uh, about two weeks into the role, I think, uh, Yasin who's joined us from, uh, she's our new general counsel, um, reading up on our, uh, updated legislation.

So, um, uh, everyone's pleased to be here. We'll just draw on people as we need and we can, um, turn to opening remarks if you are. Absolutely. Yeah. Good to go. Yep. Yeah, so look, won't dwell too long. I think that, uh, picking up point ravine made, we were stood up, uh, uh, four and a bit years ago, uh, in the way that Havelock North Contamination event to, to do a job.

And that was really to, um, uh, to give confidence to households and communities that their water is safe to drink. And so, you know, I'm really pleased that, that we have been able to make huge headway into that job. So around about three quarters of a million Kiwis, uh, were drinking water from public supplies that did not have the basic treatment and, and, and barriers in place.

We saw in Queenstown a few, uh, a couple of years back, the crypto security outbreak. What happens if you don't? Um, and, and the team's focused on, on getting that number down. So we're now under 300,000 people, um, uh, with that number. So we're well over halfway, and we would anticipate getting that down to zero within the next, uh, 12 to 18 months.

There's still more work to do after that, of course, just making sure those, uh, things continue to work. And we've got issues with resilience that we know about, uh, where, where, um, uh, treatment plan gets taken out, what, what happens next. But really the focus for us now broadens out as an organization into, uh, the water infrastructure, water services space more generally.

The, the reforms, uh, are set, the legislation is in place, and so we are looking to prioritize, uh, introducing the tools that will support an effective and efficient system. So, uh, late last year, um, uh, uh, Sarah let the, the work with industry to set first ever, uh, national waste border standards.

So they will replace existing, uh, resource consents as they expire, and, and people will move to a standard of discharge that meets public health and environmental norms, uh, and doesn't, uh, uh, doesn't overco it, doesn't undercook it. And, and will get to a, um, a far more efficient system, probably strip of nearly a billion dollars, uh, about 800, uh, million, uh, to a billion out of just the consenting process alone.

And of course, there is the standardization of infrastructure. So there are more tools for us to build, uh, engineering design standards and so on. So we, we look forward to working with operators, suppliers, industry o on, on how we can standardize infrastructure and strip some cost out while, uh, fixing the infrastructure we know needs work.

Thank you so much. We do have some questions from, uh, members. So we'll start with Andy, then Tanya, and Good to see you. You too. Um, that's the first place I wanted to start is the consenting area. It's just the standards that have been applied, the sense of those standards. Can you just give us a little bit of a spell out of how bad different to where they were?

Mm-hmm. And, um, you talked about that saving, and I counsels the complaint. To me, the cost of consenting mm-hmm. Um, was gonna be more than the cost of the plan was. Yeah. Um, which, you know, gets the full standard completely nothing standards. Mm-hmm. Um, but can you spell that out and what that looks like, and, and maybe give us some illustrations of where the savings potentially for some communities will be missing. Yeah.

So look, in a general, uh, in a, in an overview sense, we have about 320, um, public, uh, waste water treatment plants operated by councils. And shortly a number of water entities across the country. There has been a, um, uh, a backing up of, uh, expired consents for some time now.

And, and you know, about a third of those plants are operating on expired consent, some of 'em as long as 20 years. So, so that's a pretty big indicator that there's a problem. And, and when recon consenting is happening, it's not being built a a And I think some of the reason for that is by the time you get to the other side of the consenting process, the cost for the community is rejected.

And so, one, one of the things we look to do with, uh, stand, uh, national standard for wastewater is look at what is the public health, um, bar that you need to treat for so that people don't get sick when it's discharged? What is the, uh, the reasonable investment in, in mitigating the environmental impact of discharging into the environment, whether it's land, uh, fresh water or sea.

And, and so we used the best technical experts we could in the industry to answer those questions and, and standardize it. But the way that we'll move from the old system, which is case by case and in fact not really enforced and monitored very well and not working, is when your consent lapses, you apply, uh, uh, for a new consent, uh, with the standard that you, um, that is applicable.

So if you're discharging into fresh water, there's a fresh water standard depending on type of freshwater if you are to see or to land. And if you, if your application meets the standard, the regional council, the consenting authority must issue a consent. And, and, and that consent will be, uh, 30 or 30, 35 years, 35 year duration.

So there's some certainty of investment for the operator and the rate payers paying for it. So you'll go to a much more certain, and you know, what you have to apply for where it's currently until you're on the other side of the consent process, you Don't, so you're confident that when you go for, you say, look, this will meet the standard. That's the standard which required for effectively you almost activity.

That's right. That's right. Okay. That, that's very helpful. Second question is, can you just, um, talk us through the, um, issues around the size of the jurisdiction? You've got, uh, originally I think under three waters that went right down to about two houses, something like that, uh, at one stage. Yeah. Um, so you're referring to drinking primarily? Yeah.

So what, what stage of community That's right. Do you go to, yeah. So, so, um, the, uh, the drinking water regime was amended last year to, to change the threshold of supplies that would come under regulation. Um, we, we are still, uh, we have a period of transition for supplies to come on board into the regime.

So initially about 2000 supplies that were on the register with Ministry of Health transitioned over to us that included all of the council supplies, that service about 85% of the country, um, and a and a number of small private community supplies that probably had accessed the subsidy back in the day. And, and he, and hence were on the register, um, we, we have, uh, a number of small supplies that must register by 2028 and comply by 2030.

Uh, but the, um, the supplies that service very small communities up to, uh, 25, uh, people, uh, are not required to register. Um, and so that's, uh, off often arrangements between neighbors. You might have something in a farming, uh, area, um, uh, but like those communities, we can still support with guidance and information.

Um, but they are not required, um, to come in under the regime. Phil, thank you. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you. Um, Madam Chair, thank you for, for your time. Um, you talk about confident providing confidence to households, uh, for water services. Um, people in Wellington at the moment will have some concerns about whether that's, uh, the case or not.

Uh, and clearly there's a transitional phase at the moment, but what is the extent of involvement for the regulator right here, right now, and what is, uh, unfolding here locally? Yeah, So our role in waste and storm, I would categorize it as a, um, oversight of the system of, um, wastewater and stormwater services.

Is, is it working as a system. Um, and so in relation to my point, uh, which has been playing out over the last week or so, our, our interest is, is not necessarily a direct one that's for, um, Wellington Water and then Greater Wellington as, as the, uh, frontline regulator. And they issue the consent for that discharge and, and they're required to monitor the performance of the plant and type enforcement action is, uh, perhaps is underway.

So, uh, our role is, is to look at the system, and that includes the regulators and, and look at, um, whether they are doing the job, uh, meant to do. We also have a couple of direct tools to support the performance of the system. So standards I mentioned before, we can set the standards that can help that system perform.

That will actually give us, um, that strips out a large amount of, um, uh, consent conditions that currently operate. You can have up to a hundred of them. They're usually ignored. They're not monitored and not necessarily reported on. We'll have the key metrics only and they'll be nationally consistent. So I think we'll get more transparency for the public and community.

As a result, we get more consistency in enforcement and monitoring practices as a result. We can also set targets. So, uh, uh, right now the bit the public usually sees is when wastewater is, uh, overflows. So it might come into a beach, uh, in Auckland and summer, or it might overflow, uh, in the network.

And, uh, that will happen. Uh, what we want to do though, is bring it in into the light. 'cause half of the country's overflow networks are not con uh, overflows are not consented at all. They're pretended to not happen. Uh, so everyone will be required to consent overflows they, and report on to the public. So transparency and targets.

And, and third, thirdly, we have the ability to authorize or license the credentials of the workforce that operate the treatment plants and work on the network. Is that, And that's across, That's across retrospectively consent. 'cause some of these events happen before, that's right.

Before they, like, by accident. So how could you consent That? So these are new, these are new tools. So in the overflow space, uh, that standard got issued last year, and so you could talk To them. Yeah. So at the moment, about half of, so some, uh, regions have consents in place to allow a certain number of overflows.

Yeah. So, uh, because they happen obviously. And so, and then they will have a management plan about how you try and reduce the risk and what you do when you have an overflow. And in some places they're prohibited, which means they're completely invisible. So we have no reporting of them. The public is unlikely to get notifications.

So what we are putting in place is requirement that everyone has consenting and everyone is reporting and notifying the public. Mm-hmm. Um, which will be a real change. So the bar is quite low at the moment. So this is the first step. And then we could look at what else we can do in the future. Yeah. What we're looking to do is have operators, um, get the, get the overflow happening in a place that's best on the network and not, not somewhere where you shouldn't have the overflow happening.

And, and, and so I I stop that. We could, yeah. Yes. Great. It'll, it'll rain. We'll Just, um, we'll just, we'll just, uh, go to tangy and he'll finish his question, and then we've gotta start from Tim, and then we'll go to Tam. Some primary reason. Yeah. Thanks. So just if I can move to sort of affordability and household costs sort of issues, which is, um, pretty rife at the moment.

Have, have you done any sort of analysis of the likely impact on, uh, household bills when it comes to quarter costs as a result of the government's reforms? And I guess particularly in areas where there might be, um, smaller communities or limited, uh, economies of scale compared to what was previously, uh, proposed?

Yeah, so, so our, our legislation is premised and on and requires of us, uh, a proportionate risk-based, uh, approach to regulation. So, um, you know, that recognizes that big, um, urban areas and cities with large populations have a different risk profile if, if contamination comes in than, uh, small rural communities.

We put, as a result of that, direct result of that, uh, we, we put quite a lot of work over the last 18 months into how, how do you treat water safely in, uh, particularly rural communities and in small supplies where the economics of a central treatment plant for a small number of rate payers and customers are ugly.

Uh, a very high cost. And so we, uh, as a result of that, we have issued, uh, a number of what are called acceptable solutions, uh, for a technology called endpoint treatment, where, where those communities can have treated water at the site of their house with a unit that's a little bit like a, a a, a gas run cylinder, if you like, attached to the house.

Um, and, and the economics of that are, are comparable to those in an urban area, um, the central treatment plant, and a large number of customers. So that's an example of, uh, we, we are aware that cost and affordability are, are real and, and that, uh, different circumstances, um, can warrant a different response and technology Have, have you got though any information on expected ranges as to what that would look like?

I get the point you're making about comparability between small, rural, rural communities and urban ones, but I'm more interested in the costs implications for households. So If I may, I think it's a very good point. So as an approach, what we are relying heavily on as a regulator, we'll be putting regulations in place, but at the same time, we are also pushing for standardization.

So we are saying if the standardization certainty, the cost should come down. Um, so we are pushing for standardization, and as Alan just mentioned, the wastewater, um, discharge tenders from treatment plants is gonna reduce costs when we put standards for wastewater engineering design standards, which we are working on next.

So the next thing the organization will release over the next 12 to 18 months would be, uh, design standards for stormwater water supply and wastewater. So that right across the country, there's only one set of, uh, design standards. At the moment. Every local government has got their own standards. So developers and contractors have to, um, get themselves geared up for all these varying standards.

So standardization should reduce cost, uh, it should reduce design cost, it should reduce procurement cost, it should reduce operating and maintenance costs. So we have some of these other initiatives underway to make sure we give effect to this proportionality that we talk about.

But do you hold any advice or modeling on the actual projected quantums? I get you as a response, but I'm talking about the actual raw figures that households will face as a result of This change. So, so, um, tho as a system, no, that, that, that information's more likely to sit with, uh, department of Internal Affairs firstly, and then as they come on board the commerce commission, which is looking at the asset health condition. Yeah.

Thank you. Sorry. So when you can see, or if you can see that, that it's, that I think what I said was trains going off the rails or it's, it's getting off track that the money's not going there and that you can see the inevitable was going to happen. Do you have a role in that? Can you intervene in this case? Could you have intervened?

Or do you just have to sit back and watch that happen? So We, we don't have, uh, the, the role or, or powers to intervene in a, in a, in a wastewater treatment plants, uh, performance, uh, directly. That is the, the role of the regional council up and down the country. And, and as I indicated earlier, that's quite variable, um, historically, and we hope standards and, and, and, and some of the setup, uh, that we've got going forward will improve the situation.

What what we are looking for though is systemic things. So if there are, I mean, we don't know. But we are aware that there are some systemic issues, uh, with, with waste water across the country that, that go across operators.

And so, uh, one of those is the, the, the pipes that serve, uh, wastewater treatment of plants and, and in fact in drinking water as well, collectively, that's about 80% of the whole three waters asset. So, so when you talk about cost and what is the cost to come, a lot of it is sitting there and it's underground and difficult to know what condition it's in.

And, and, uh, while councils are pretty good knowing the condition of drinking water pipes for obvious reasons, uh, the information base on, on wastewater reticulation is, uh, poorer and, and often unknown. And, and so that's a system concern that we have been highlighting and we're really looking forward to the Commerce Commission standing up its role, which is to, to collect information on the condition of the asset, the investment going in the sufficiency of that investment and, and the efficiency of that investment.

So yes, we, we have a role and with that role we can, um, advise and we can make recommendations a, about issues we see in the system. So if you see the next one Happening, yes, We, uh, that's right. So we have to set up a, a way to monitor whether the system's forming as it's expected, which is what we are, um, doing now. 'cause we can see that that's about to create a massive leak into an ocean that you, you refer to beaches in Auckland in drinking water. We can, we've got record of tools, uh, yeah. spilling sewage into, into there as well. So is it only on regional councils when there is a nationwide problem with this French multinational company who is, um, polluting our environment without reprimand? Who, who, what tools would you need to fully hold them to account given that the scale is nationwide?

Yeah, so just to talk to the, to the, the point about regional councils are making isn't that it's all on all all on them per se. It, it's to, to clarify their role as the, as the frontline environmental regulator of those discharges. And it's their responsibility to monitor them and take action if they need to.

Um, it, it, you are right about Veolia. And we also have, uh, city care, uh, which is owned by Christchurch City Council as a another major provider, for example, uh, uh, of similar services across the country. So the key point probably to be made on that front is whatever model you use and, and, and contracting, uh, uh, expertise is, is, uh, is absolutely fine and relatively common practice here and overseas.

You, you need to be able to, uh, manage that contract and the relationship and the, and the expectations and accountabilities with a contracted party. Well, and, and so, um, I, I don't have a comment to make on, on Veolia specifically at all, but I would say, uh, whatever model you're using, you need to have the capability to, to, to utilize that, that mechanism and manage the oversight of it. Mm.

Because what I, I would just, sorry, add to, I think the, the core to your question, and I think the earlier question is, well, what is our role? And o the overriding role is that we provide leadership to the industry, that we give trust and confidence to the consumers that their water supply is safe to drink, and that we are looking after the environment from which the water is taken.

And, and therefore, even though we may not have the direct, uh, teeth, so to speak, I believe it is our role to monitor, to identify, and then to influence and push the other agencies failing, which to bring that to the attention of the minister that we have these issues.

And that is exactly what we have been doing in recent times when there were overflows in Mai in Auckland, uh, we were in contact with the minister and saying to the minister, we don't have the powers, but other agencies do. And if they're not, um, lifting their game, you know, we made it known and other agencies did lift their game, including Auckland Council, which was the first regulator and then the EPA.

Mm-hmm. So to answer your question, I would say that, uh, Tamar, ROI, water Services Authority has that role. We will, uh, lift our gain. These are early days. We've been focusing on drinking water, we are focusing on wastewater. Um, we will make it our business to improve the water industry in New Zealand.

And I, sorry, I just have two, um, follow up and I'll put them together. So maybe you can answer them both in the same answer. The first is, um, when obviously there is a restructure of local government being explored at the moment with the possibility that we won't have regional council soon. So what thinking is Toma UA doing towards who will take that role on afterwards?

And my second question is, uh, you made a comment about, um, not being popular to the public to invest or, um, that sometimes things like water, uh, don't get the funding that they need because they're not the kind of, I'm guessing what you meant is that they're not the bright, shiny things that people will see immediately or see at all.

Um, maybe if that's what you meant is that I I you might be referring to my point that that, um, you know, 8% of the, the assets that's under the ground, um, hit hidden from view, and we don't necessarily know what conditions it's in. I, I don't think, uh, That's okay then. If that's how you're coming, that's fine. We'll leave that.

Um, what I wanna know is, um, we, on Wellington City Council, we gave 208 million in the long term plan to the more point treatment, um, center that is failing currently. Do you know what, let's not go there, but we'll come to you after, after us, we'll, we'll come to you.

What is going wrong in the pipeline where there is money being allocated by councils? Where is it going wrong? And that there's still these failures happening despite there being in invest more investment than previous councils. We can agree on that. We, we, what's go, where's it going wrong in the pipeline? Who's not delivering? Where are the problems happening?

So, so, so that's, you know, one of the really encouraging things from the, the last few years and the reforms that we have now got is agencies charged with answering, uh, that question. And we've also got responsibilities and disciplines on the owners and operators of water services to, to, to be able to, um, account, uh, for the use of the money as well.

So prior to that, um, we, we probably didn't have the level of information and, and scrutiny, uh, on these services. It's a big asset. Um, it's an important asset that we need. So Commerce Commission is standing up, uh, and, and they are going to be collecting the information on the investment and, and, uh, comparing how much it costs, uh, to operate these services, uh, across the country.

And, and, and looking at the efficiency of that spend and whether, uh, it's going on the right stuff. Uh, there's a requirement to ring fence money now raised for water services that, uh, they must be spent on water services, not on other things. And, and, and that's a change as well. So I think we're getting some of the fundamentals in place, and, and as the information gets better, we we'll know what the size of the challenge is and, and, uh, you know, how long it'll take communities to Get there. Question and the regional council question. So, uh, aware of, uh, the proposals out there, uh, for changes, uh, there, that there, there will need to be, uh, a, a set of responsibilities carried out for environmental, um, uh, regulation management that's been carried out through the RMA reforms.

There's legislation in the house at the moment, and, and we work, uh, generally with the Department of Internal Affairs, uh, and, and ministry for the environment, um, to support their advice functions. We go through that. Uh, it, it, it's unique in that we have 16 regional councils.
